title: Dry Weekly Market Monitor
summary:
Main
Week 51 comes to confirm that the sentiment of December is supportive for an upward push of rates in the smaller ship sizes. Few days before Christmas, Capesize rates fell to the lowest level of one month, however, the demand trend line keeps an optimistic trend for an upcoming rebound. In the Panamax segment, the market experiences the same downward direction and the spike comes from the smallest ship size segment, the handysize.

children.title: Main
children.summary: Week 51 comes to confirm that the sentiment of December is supportive for an upward push of rates in the smaller ship sizes. Few days before Christmas, Capesize rates fell to the lowest level of one month, however, the demand trend line keeps an optimistic trend for an upcoming rebound.

children.title: SECTION 1 - FREIGHT - Market Rates ($/t) - Weaker
children.summary:
‘The Big Picture' - Capesize and Panamax Bulkers and Smaller Ship Sizes
Image reference: ch1.png | assets/2021-12-22_dry-weekly-market-monitor_img_ch1_0692ffa70397.png
Following the highs recorded at Week 49 for Capesize ships, the momentum was weakened for two consecutive weeks with Brazil to NChina rates now heading below $23/ton, however, the market remains healthy compared to December 2020. In the Panamax segment, the decreasing trend is even more pronounced with Cont-Far East rates at less than $45/t, while it seems a challenge for the market to fetch again levels in the range of $50/t.

children.title: SECTION 2 - SUPPLY - Ballasters View Number of Vessels - Decreasing
section_path: SECTION 2 - SUPPLY - Ballasters View Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.section_path_text: SECTION 2 - SUPPLY - Ballasters View Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.summary:
Supply Trend Lines for Key Load Areas
Image reference: ch2.png | assets/2021-12-22_dry-weekly-market-monitor_img_ch2_de187638418a.png
The supply trend line has kept the decreasing trend from Week 49 up to now as we mentioned in our previous Weekly Monitor. However, this week comes with a significant spike in the handysize NOPAC area.

children.title: Increasing for Supramax
children.summary:
Evolution of Demand -% Growth
Image reference: ch3.png | assets/2021-12-22_dry-weekly-market-monitor_img_ch3_59441311fddb.png
The opening of the third week of December comes to confirm that the increasing trend in ton days demand growth remains in the supramax segment. In the Capesize segment, the momentum has now started to decrease with challenges from the Chinese economy and levels of steel production during December.

children.title: SECTION 4 - CHINESE PORT CONGESTIONS - Number of Vessels - Decreasing
section_path: SECTION 4 - CHINESE PORT CONGESTIONS - Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.section_path_text: SECTION 4 - CHINESE PORT CONGESTIONS - Number of Vessels - Decreasing
children.summary:
Dry bulk ships congested around Chinese ports
Image reference: ch4.png | assets/2021-12-22_dry-weekly-market-monitor_img_ch4_b51bfe5ab752.png
Week 51 keeps the decreasing trend below the average trend line of previous weeks, however, we see a sense of upward trend stemming from the smaller ship sizes. In the Capesize segment, the number of ships congested around Chinese ports has decreased by 20% compared to the levels of ten weeks ago, whereas in the handysize segment, we notice similar levels of congestion.
